title: Sending Data — POST Requests
subtitle: GET fetches. POST creates. Learn to send data to a server.

sections.content:
So far you've only used `GET` — asking for data. But you also need to **send** data: create a user, submit a form, post a comment.

That's what `POST` is for.

In curl:
- `-X POST` sets the method to POST
- `-H "Content-Type: application/json"` tells the server what format you're sending
- `-d '{...}'` is the request body — the data you're sending
keyPoints: Always include `Content-Type: application/json` when sending JSON

sections.instruction: Here's the anatomy of a POST request in curl.

blocks.label: POST with JSON body
blocks.code:
curl -X POST https://api.example.com/todos \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"text": "Learn curl", "done": false}'

sections.explanation:
Line by line:
`-X POST` — use POST method instead of GET
`-H "Content-Type: application/json"` — tell the server the body is JSON
`-d '...'` — the body data (use single quotes to avoid shell escaping issues with double quotes inside)

sections.instruction: First, GET the current list of todos to see what's there.

onSuccess.message: Two todos. Now let's add a third one using POST.

sections.instruction: Now POST a new todo to the server. Send it as a JSON body.
sections.prompt: Type `curl -X POST https://mock.zero2claude.dev/todos -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"text": "Learn curl", "done": false}'`
sections.hint: Start with curl -X POST, then the URL, then the -H header, then -d with the JSON body in single quotes

onSuccess.message:
The server responded with the created todo — including an `id` it assigned, a `createdAt` timestamp, and the data you sent back. This confirms the item was saved.

This is the exact pattern used by every API: POST data → server creates resource → server returns the created object with its new ID.

sections.question: You want to add a new product to an e-commerce API. The API docs say `POST /api/products`. Which curl command is correct?
sections.explanation: POST requests send data in the body (with -d), not in the URL. You need -X POST to change the method, Content-Type to tell the server the format, and -d with the JSON payload. Option A uses query params which is wrong for creation. Options C and D are invalid.

completionMessage: GET reads. POST creates. You now know both directions of the HTTP conversation — asking for data and sending data.
